standards.title: Password Security Requirements
standards.description: OWASP ASVS V2.1 defines comprehensive password security requirements for application developers. Passwords SHALL be at least 12 characters for user-set passwords and at least 6 characters for system-generated temporary passwords. Maximum length SHALL be at least 128 characters. All Unicode characters SHALL be accepted including spaces. Password composition rules (requiring specific character types) SHALL NOT be enforced as they reduce entropy and user experience. Passwords SHALL be checked against a set of at least 10 million known breached passwords. Password hints and knowledge-based recovery questions SHALL NOT be used. Password change SHALL require the current password. These requirements align with NIST SP 800-63B and represent current best practices for password security.

standards.title: General Authenticator Security
standards.description: OWASP ASVS V2.2 covers general authenticator security requirements applicable to all authentication mechanisms. Anti-automation controls SHALL be in place to mitigate credential testing, brute force, and account lockout attacks. The use of weak authenticators (email as a factor, SMS as a sole factor) SHALL be restricted based on risk assessment. Authentication credentials SHALL be stored using approved cryptographic techniques (bcrypt, scrypt, Argon2, PBKDF2 with high iteration count). Failed authentication responses SHALL not indicate which part of the authentication failed (username or password). Rate limiting SHALL be enforced across all authentication endpoints. Credential recovery SHALL not reveal whether an account exists.

standards.title: Credential Storage Requirements
standards.description: OWASP ASVS V2.4 specifies how credentials must be stored to resist offline attacks. Passwords SHALL be stored using a one-way key derivation function that is salted and computationally expensive. At ASVS Level 2, the approved algorithms are bcrypt (minimum cost factor 10), scrypt (minimum parameters N=2^14, r=8, p=1), Argon2id (minimum parameters m=19456, t=2, p=1), or PBKDF2 (minimum 310,000 iterations with HMAC-SHA256). The salt SHALL be at least 16 bytes and generated using a cryptographically secure random number generator. Passwords SHALL NOT be stored using fast hash algorithms (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256 without key stretching). If pepper is used, it SHALL be stored separately from the password hashes in a hardware security module or secure enclave.

standards.title: Credential Recovery Requirements
standards.description: OWASP ASVS V2.5 defines requirements for secure credential recovery processes. Account recovery SHALL not reveal whether an account exists (to prevent user enumeration). Recovery tokens SHALL have a minimum of 20 bits of entropy and SHALL expire after a short time period (maximum 20 minutes). Recovery tokens SHALL be single-use. Knowledge-based authentication (security questions) SHALL NOT be used as they are easily guessable or researched. Recovery SHALL require possession of a verified recovery channel (email, phone) or a pre-registered authenticator. At ASVS Level 3, recovery SHALL require a combination of a verified channel and a pre-registered authenticator, or require identity proofing equivalent to the account's original enrollment.

standards.title: Out-of-Band Verifier Requirements
standards.description: OWASP ASVS V2.7 covers security requirements for out-of-band authentication verifiers such as push notifications, SMS codes, and voice calls. Out-of-band authentication SHALL use a secondary communication channel that is independent of the primary authentication channel. OTP codes delivered via the secondary channel SHALL have a minimum of 6 digits, SHALL expire within 10 minutes, and SHALL be usable only once. The verifier SHALL implement rate limiting on OTP verification attempts (maximum 5 attempts per code). PSTN-based delivery (SMS/voice) is classified as restricted and SHALL be accompanied by guidance to users about its limitations. Push notification-based authentication with number matching is preferred over SMS delivery.

standards.title: One-Time Verifier Requirements
standards.description: OWASP ASVS V2.8 covers security requirements for time-based and event-based one-time password (OTP) authenticators. Time-based OTPs SHALL use a time step of 30 or 60 seconds and the verifier SHALL accept OTPs within a defined drift window (typically one time step before and after the current period). The shared secret SHALL have at least 128 bits of entropy and SHALL be generated using a cryptographically secure random number generator. Symmetric keys used for OTP generation SHALL be protected using a tamper-resistant device or secure enclave where available. Look-up recovery codes SHALL have at least 112 bits of entropy and SHALL be stored using approved one-way functions.

standards.title: Cryptographic Verifier Requirements
standards.description: OWASP ASVS V2.9 covers security requirements for cryptographic authenticators including FIDO2/WebAuthn security keys and platform authenticators. Cryptographic verifiers SHALL validate that the assertion is signed by the expected authenticator. Attestation SHALL be verified when required by the organization's risk assessment. The verifier SHALL verify that the origin and channel ID in the assertion match the expected values to prevent relay attacks. At ASVS Level 3, only hardware-backed cryptographic authenticators that provide verifier impersonation resistance SHALL be accepted. The authenticator SHALL generate a new key pair for each registration to prevent cross-service tracking. WebAuthn is the preferred implementation for cryptographic authentication in web applications.

standards.title: Service Authentication Requirements
standards.description: OWASP ASVS V2.10 covers authentication requirements for service-to-service communication and API authentication. Service accounts SHALL NOT use static credentials that are shared in code, configuration files, or environment variables without appropriate secret management. API keys, if used, SHALL NOT be the sole authentication mechanism for sensitive operations. Service-to-service authentication SHALL use mutual TLS (mTLS), OAuth 2.0 client credentials with short-lived tokens, or equivalent cryptographic mechanisms. Secrets SHALL be stored in dedicated secret management systems (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault) with automatic rotation. Service account passwords SHALL be at least 32 characters of random data when password-based authentication is unavoidable.

standards.title: Fundamental Session Management Security
standards.description: OWASP ASVS V3.1 defines fundamental session management requirements. Session tokens SHALL NOT be exposed in URL parameters (to prevent leakage through referer headers and server logs). The application SHALL generate a new session token upon successful authentication, effectively invalidating any pre-authentication session tokens. Session tokens SHALL be generated using a cryptographically secure random number generator with at least 128 bits of entropy. Session management SHALL be implemented using well-vetted frameworks rather than custom session management code. Session tokens SHALL be transmitted only over encrypted channels (TLS) and SHALL have the Secure, HttpOnly, and SameSite cookie attributes set.

standards.title: Session Binding Requirements
standards.description: OWASP ASVS V3.2 requires that sessions are properly bound to the authenticated user and the security context of the authentication event. The application SHALL generate a new session upon authentication and SHALL NOT reuse an existing session. Session tokens SHALL be bound to the user and the authentication method used. At ASVS Level 2, stateful session tokens SHALL be validated server-side on every request. Stateless tokens (JWTs) SHALL include audience, issuer, and expiration claims that are validated on every request. At ASVS Level 3, session tokens SHALL additionally be bound to the TLS connection or client certificate to prevent session token theft and replay from a different context.

standards.title: Session Timeout Requirements
standards.description: OWASP ASVS V3.3 defines session timeout and logout requirements. Sessions SHALL have an idle timeout that terminates the session after a defined period of inactivity (maximum 30 minutes for standard applications, shorter for sensitive applications). Sessions SHALL have an absolute timeout that terminates the session regardless of activity (maximum 12 hours for standard, 4 hours for sensitive). Upon logout, the session SHALL be fully invalidated on the server side — for stateless tokens, a token revocation mechanism (blocklist) SHALL be implemented. All authentication tokens and session tokens SHALL be invalidated upon logout. The application SHALL provide a visible, easily accessible logout function on every authenticated page.

standards.title: Cookie-Based Session Management
standards.description: OWASP ASVS V3.4 defines security requirements for cookie-based session management. Session cookies SHALL set the Secure attribute to prevent transmission over unencrypted connections. Session cookies SHALL set the HttpOnly attribute to prevent JavaScript access (mitigating XSS-based session theft). Session cookies SHALL set the SameSite attribute to Lax or Strict to prevent cross-site request forgery. The cookie path SHALL be set to the most restrictive path possible. The cookie domain SHALL not be set to a parent domain that could allow sibling applications to access the session. Cookie names SHALL NOT reveal the technology stack or framework in use. These cookie security attributes provide defense-in-depth against session hijacking.

standards.title: Token-Based Session Management
standards.description: OWASP ASVS V3.5 defines security requirements for token-based session management (JWTs, OAuth tokens). Stateless tokens SHALL include audience (aud), issuer (iss), issued-at (iat), and expiration (exp) claims, and these SHALL be validated on every request. The token signature SHALL be verified using a trusted public key or shared secret. Tokens SHALL use RS256, ES256, or EdDSA algorithms — the none algorithm and symmetric algorithms with publicly known keys SHALL be rejected. Token lifetimes SHALL be minimized (access tokens: 5-15 minutes, refresh tokens: hours-days). Refresh token rotation SHALL be implemented so that each refresh token can only be used once. Token revocation mechanisms SHALL exist for immediate session termination.

standards.title: Defenses Against Session Management Exploits
standards.description: OWASP ASVS V3.7 covers defenses against common session management attacks. The application SHALL invalidate all existing sessions when a user changes their password, updates their MFA configuration, or reports suspicious activity. Concurrent session limits SHOULD be enforced based on risk assessment. The application SHALL detect and respond to session fixation attacks by generating new session identifiers after authentication. The application SHALL provide users with the ability to view and terminate their active sessions from other devices or locations. These controls ensure that session management cannot be exploited to maintain persistent unauthorized access even after the legitimate user has taken remedial action.

standards.title: General Access Control Design
standards.description: OWASP ASVS V4.1 defines general access control design requirements. Access control SHALL be enforced on the server side (or in a trusted service layer) where it cannot be bypassed by the client. All access control decisions SHALL deny by default — access SHALL be explicitly granted rather than implicitly available. Access control logic SHALL be centralized in a single component or layer rather than distributed throughout the application code, to ensure consistency and simplify auditing. The access control mechanism SHALL enforce access policies based on the principle of least privilege. Access control failures SHALL be logged and SHALL alert administrators when patterns suggest an attack (repeated authorization failures from a single user or IP).

standards.title: Operation Level Access Control
standards.description: OWASP ASVS V4.2 requires access control enforcement at the operation level. Users SHALL only be able to access functions and services for which they possess specific authorization. The application SHALL enforce access control on every API endpoint, not just at the UI level. Direct object references SHALL be protected — the application SHALL verify that the user is authorized to access the specific object they are requesting (preventing insecure direct object reference vulnerabilities). Administrative functions SHALL be accessible only from administrative interfaces. The application SHALL protect against vertical privilege escalation (regular users accessing admin functions) and horizontal privilege escalation (users accessing other users' data) through consistent server-side authorization checks.

standards.title: Other Access Control Considerations
standards.description: OWASP ASVS V4.3 covers additional access control requirements including administrative interface protection and directory traversal prevention. Administrative interfaces SHALL use multi-factor authentication and SHALL be restricted to authorized administrative networks or IP ranges. The application SHALL not allow directory traversal or path manipulation to access files or resources outside of the intended scope. Cross-site request forgery (CSRF) protections SHALL be implemented for all state-changing operations. The application SHALL verify that the user has explicit authorization for bulk operations, data export, and API access to prevent mass data exfiltration. These controls address common access control bypasses that occur when authorization is only enforced at the primary access path.
